notes.curated: Dracarys Llama 3.1 70B Instruct is AbacusAI's August 2024 fine-tune of Llama 3.1 70B Instruct, specifically optimized for complex instruction following, multi-step tool use, and agentic task completion. AbacusAI applied their proprietary supervised fine-tuning and DPO pipeline targeting enterprise assistant use cases. Community evaluations found it superior to the base Llama 3.1 70B Instruct on agentic benchmarks (ToolBench, AgentBench) while maintaining general chat quality. Best deployed in structured agentic pipelines; less differentiated for general creative or open-ended tasks compared to the base model.

7B MPT-architecture instruction-tuned LLM pre-trained on 980B tokens with ~13% SEA language coverage, supporting English, Chinese, Indonesian, Malay, Thai, Vietnamese, Filipino, Tamil, Burmese, Khmer, and Lao. Fine-tuned on ~200K Indonesian instruction pairs; the first Southeast Asian-focused open LLM, released January 2024.

Evo 2 is a 40-billion-parameter genomic foundation model trained on 9.3 trillion nucleotides spanning prokaryotes, eukaryotes, and viruses, using the StripedHyena SSM architecture. It takes raw DNA sequences as input and outputs predictions for variant effects, gene essentiality, and regulatory function, or generates novel sequences at genome scale. Compared to earlier Evo 1 and ESM-based protein-only models, Evo 2 operates across the full central dogma and handles sequences up to 1 million tokens in a single context window.

1024-dimensional multilingual embedding model supporting 100+ languages via dense, sparse, and multi-vector (ColBERT) retrieval in a single model. Supports inputs up to 8192 tokens and set new state-of-the-art results on the multilingual MTEB retrieval tracks at release in early 2024. Highly competitive on BEIR and cross-lingual benchmarks.

StarCoder2 7B is BigCode's February 2024 code model trained on 3.5T tokens of The Stack v2 (619 programming languages), using a 16K context window with multi-query attention and Fill-in-the-Middle support. It outperforms the original StarCoder 15B and Code Llama 7B on most coding benchmarks despite its smaller size, owing to the improved data quality of The Stack v2 and better training recipe. Particularly strong on Python, JavaScript, and systems languages. FIM makes it excellent for IDE completion. Now competes with DeepSeek Coder 6.7B; slightly behind on HumanEval but stronger on multilingual code tasks.

12B-parameter distilled (latent adversarial diffusion distillation) FLUX.1 variant generating high-quality images in 1–4 steps; inference in ~1–2 seconds on RTX 4090. Released under Apache 2.0 for personal, scientific, and commercial use. Trades some fine detail for extreme speed.

4B-parameter rectified flow transformer unifying text-to-image generation and multi-reference editing in a single compact architecture, capable of end-to-end inference in under one second. Runs on consumer GPUs with ~13GB VRAM; released under Apache 2.0 in early 2026.

12B-parameter rectified flow transformer for text-to-image generation, trained with guidance distillation for quality near FLUX.1 [pro]. Requires ~16GB VRAM; licensed for non-commercial research and development use. Strong prompt adherence and photorealistic quality.

12B-parameter FLUX.1 Kontext model for in-context image generation and editing: accepts text + reference images to support inpainting, character/object consistency, and style transfer. FP8-quantised NIM reduces size to 12GB, FP4 to 7GB; non-commercial dev licence. Announced May 2025.

Seed-OSS-36B-Instruct is a 36B causal transformer (RoPE, GQA, RMSNorm, SwiGLU) from ByteDance's Seed Team, released August 20, 2025 under Apache 2.0, trained on 12T tokens with a native 512K context window. It achieves 91.7% on AIME24 and 65% on BeyondAIME (both reported as open-source SOTA at release), 67.4 on LiveCodeBench v6, and 94.6 on RULER at 128K context. Features a user-controllable Thinking Budget parameter for adjustable chain-of-thought length.

ColabFold MSA Search is a fast multiple-sequence-alignment search service that replaces the hours-long HHblits/JackHMMER pipeline used by AlphaFold2 with MMseqs2-based search against a clustered UniRef and environmental-sequence database. Input is one or more protein sequences; output is a paired or unpaired MSA in A3M format ready for AlphaFold2 or RoseTTAFold. It reduces alignment time from hours to seconds, making large-scale or interactive structure prediction practical.

AlphaFold2 is DeepMind's landmark protein structure prediction model that accepts an amino-acid sequence and multiple-sequence alignment and outputs all-atom 3D coordinates with per-residue confidence (pLDDT) and predicted aligned error. It achieves near-experimental accuracy on single-chain proteins as demonstrated in CASP14, surpassing all prior computational methods. The PDB-wide AF2 database covering 200M+ protein structures has become the standard reference for structural bioinformatics.

AlphaFold2-Multimer extends AlphaFold2 to predict the 3D structure of protein complexes by accepting multiple chains and paired inter-chain MSAs. It outputs stoichiometric complex coordinates with interface pTM (ipTM) scores for evaluating binding geometry. Compared to single-chain AF2, it introduces chain-pair MSA pairing and modified loss terms that improve interface accuracy, enabling applications in drug target identification and antibody-antigen modeling.

DeepSeek-V3.1-Terminus is a 671B MoE hybrid model (37B active) with a two-phase long-context training process extending to 128K tokens, released September 2025. It scores 85.0 on MMLU-Pro, 80.7 on GPQA-Diamond, 74.9 on LiveCodeBench, and 96.8 on SimpleQA. The model incorporates FP8 microscaling for efficient inference and supports language consistency and agent-task improvements over V3.1.

DeepSeek-V3.2 is a 671B MoE model (37B active) released December 2025, built on V3.1-Terminus with the addition of DeepSeek Sparse Attention (DSA) for improved long-context efficiency. Performance is on par with V3.1-Terminus on most short and long-context benchmarks, with a 2.5-point drop on HMMT 2025 noted as the most significant regression. The Speciale variant (same base) achieves gold-medal-level performance on IOI 2025 and IMO 2025 competition benchmarks.

CodeGemma 1.1 7B is an iterative update to CodeGemma 7B released April 2024, with improved instruction following in the IT variant and better FIM completion quality in the base variant. The primary improvements over 1.0 are in chat-format code generation (the IT version better handles multi-turn coding tasks) and reduced hallucination on function signatures. Still lags behind StarCoder2 15B and DeepSeek Coder 7B Instruct on competitive coding benchmarks, but is a solid choice for constrained environments that require Google's licensing terms.

CodeGemma 7B is Google's April 2024 code-specialized model fine-tuned from Gemma 7B on 500B tokens of primarily code from GitHub. It supports Fill-in-the-Middle (FIM) for code completion and comes in base and instruct variants. Benchmarks on HumanEval and MBPP placed it below DeepSeek Coder 6.7B and Code Llama 7B in some evaluations, but it has stronger general reasoning than narrow code models. Mainly used in IDE completion pipelines where the Gemma license is preferred. The 1.1 update improved instruction following.

Gemma 2 2B IT is Google's June 2024 small instruction-tuned model using a novel architecture featuring alternating local and global attention layers plus knowledge distillation from a 27B teacher. Despite its size it substantially outperformed Gemma 1 7B and was competitive with Mistral 7B Instruct on several benchmarks. Runs on CPU or any GPU with >2 GB VRAM. The distillation approach gives it punchy reasoning for a 2B model. It is now the reference small open model for latency-constrained deployments pending wider Gemma 3n adoption.

Gemma 3 27B IT is Google's March 2025 instruction-tuned dense model with 27B parameters, 128K context window, and multimodal capability (image+text). It topped several open-model leaderboards at release for its size class, besting Llama 3.3 70B on some reasoning and multilingual benchmarks while using fewer parameters. The r/LocalLLaMA community praised its low refusal rate and sharp instruction following; a common criticism is that 27B is an awkward size — too large for 16 GB single-GPU at FP16 (needs Q4), not quite as capable as 70B class. Vision quality is strong for a sub-30B model.

Gemma 3n E2B IT is the 2B effective-parameter variant of Google's Gemma 3n 'Nano' sub-family, announced at Google I/O 2025. Gemma 3n uses a novel MatFormer architecture with per-layer parameter nesting, enabling a single checkpoint to serve multiple size points efficiently. The E2B model targets on-device mobile inference (targeting Pixel and Android devices) and supports multimodal inputs. At its size it outperforms Gemma 2 2B IT on most benchmarks. Still early in community adoption as of mid-2025; expect ongoing evals.

Gemma 3n E4B IT is the 4B effective-parameter variant in the Gemma 3n Nano family using the MatFormer nested architecture, also announced at Google I/O 2025. It provides a step up in quality over E2B while remaining suitable for on-device deployment on high-end mobile hardware. Multimodal (image+text) capable. Community benchmarks as of mid-2025 place it ahead of Llama 3.2 3B and competitive with Phi-3.5 Mini on reasoning tasks, with a smaller memory footprint than its nominal parameter count suggests.

Gemma 4 31B IT (Instruct-Tuned) is Google's April 2025 flagship open model in the Gemma 4 family, featuring a hybrid dense-MoE architecture with 31B total parameters and a 128K context window with native multimodal support (text + images). On Artificial Analysis it ranks among the top open models, competitive with Llama 4 Maverick on reasoning while being smaller. Community feedback highlights strong multilingual and instruction-following quality with minimal refusals compared to earlier Gemma models. Requires ~20 GB VRAM at BF16; Q4 quants run on a single 16 GB GPU.

Gemma 7B is Google's February 2024 dense 7B base model, part of the first Gemma release using decoder-only transformer architecture with multi-head attention and RoPE, trained on 6T tokens from web, code, and math data with an 8K context. It was competitive with Llama 2 13B and Mistral 7B on academic benchmarks. Now largely superseded by Gemma 2 9B IT which uses distillation and sliding-window attention for better quality. The raw base checkpoint is mainly useful as a fine-tuning starting point for researchers wanting Google's pretraining data distribution.

PaliGemma is Google's 3B vision-language model from May 2024 combining a SigLIP-400M vision encoder with a Gemma 2B language model. It is a transfer-learning-first model: the pretrained checkpoint is designed for fine-tuning on specific vision tasks (VQA, captioning, object detection, segmentation) rather than general chat. Achieves state-of-the-art on several VQA and captioning fine-tuning benchmarks at its parameter count. Not suitable as a general-purpose multimodal assistant out of the box — requires task-specific fine-tuning; PaliGemma 2 (November 2024) improved the backbone to Gemma 2.

9B decoder-only safety classifier built on Gemma-2, detecting four harm categories: sexually explicit content, dangerous content, hate speech, and harassment. Outputs a Yes/No probability score; the 9B variant prioritises accuracy over latency for offline moderation use cases.

Binary image classifier that detects AI-generated images from generators such as Midjourney, DALL-E, and Firefly, returning per-model confidence scores. Trained on millions of synthetic and real images; a 2024 independent study found it outperforms competing models and human expert analysis.

Per-face deepfake detection model that first localises faces via bounding boxes, then assigns each a 0–1 confidence score indicating whether the face was AI-synthesised. Input: PNG/JPEG images; output: bounding boxes with deepfake confidence scores for each detected face.

ProteinMPNN (from the Institute for Protein Design) is a message-passing graph neural network for inverse folding: given a protein backbone structure as input, it outputs amino-acid sequences predicted to fold stably into that backbone. It dramatically outperforms Rosetta-based inverse folding in experimental success rates and runs in seconds per design. It is the de-facto standard sequence design module used downstream of RFdiffusion, partial-diffusion protocols, and hallucination pipelines.

RFdiffusion fine-tunes a denoising diffusion probabilistic model on top of RoseTTAFold to generate novel protein backbone structures conditioned on motifs, binding targets, or symmetry constraints. Input can be a partial structure, a binding pocket, or purely a symmetry specification; output is an all-atom backbone that can then be sequence-designed with ProteinMPNN. It has produced functional binders, enzymes, and symmetric assemblies validated experimentally, outperforming prior hallucination and fragment-based methods.

8B open-source English instruction-following LLM (Llama architecture) developed by Stanford CRFM's Marin open lab, SFT-fine-tuned from marin-8b-base for an additional 5.3B tokens. Knowledge cutoff ~July 2024; outperforms OLMo 2 on instruct evals; released under Apache 2.0.

Code Llama 70B is Meta's code-specialized model from January 2024, fine-tuned from Llama 2 70B on 500B+ tokens of code and a code-heavy text mixture. It supports infill (FIM), a 4K–100K sliding context window via RoPE extension, and comes in base, instruct, and Python variants. At release it matched or exceeded GPT-3.5 on HumanEval and led open-weight code models. Now clearly superseded by Llama 3.1/3.3 70B Instruct and DeepSeek Coder V2 for coding tasks, but still used in legacy pipelines that were tuned for its specific format.

ESM-2 (650M parameter variant) is Meta AI's protein language model trained on 250 million UniRef90 sequences with a masked-language-modeling objective. It takes an amino-acid sequence as input and outputs contextual residue embeddings used for structure prediction, function annotation, and mutational effect scoring. The 650M checkpoint offers the best accuracy-to-compute tradeoff for embedding-based tasks; larger variants (3B, 15B) are available for higher accuracy at greater cost.

ESMFold is Meta AI's end-to-end protein structure prediction model that replaces the MSA search stage entirely by using the large language model ESM-2 to encode evolutionary information directly from a single sequence. Input is a single amino-acid sequence; output is all-atom coordinates with pLDDT and PAE. It is 60x faster than AlphaFold2 at the cost of some accuracy on hard targets, making it the practical choice for proteome-scale screening where MSA search is the bottleneck.

Llama 3.1 405B Instruct is Meta's flagship dense model released July 2024, with 405 billion parameters and a 128K context window. It was the first open-weight model to rival GPT-4 class performance on MMLU, HumanEval, and MT-Bench. Requires multi-GPU setups (typically 8×80 GB H100) for FP16 inference, making local deployment impractical for most users; GGUF Q2-Q3 quants can run on 2–4×48 GB but with quality trade-offs. Strengths: broad reasoning, long-context retrieval, and coding. Weakness: massive inference cost relative to Llama 3.3 70B which often matches it.

Llama 3.1 70B Instruct is a dense 70B model from Meta's July 2024 release, adding true 128K context (via rope scaling) and multilingual support over Llama 3.0. It sits in the sweet spot for 2×24 GB or 1×48 GB GPU deployments and was the community go-to for long-context tasks before 3.3 superseded it. Benchmark scores trail GPT-4o but beat GPT-3.5 comfortably; instruction following is solid but can drift on very long multi-turn conversations. Largely superseded by Llama 3.3 70B which fixes several instruction-following regressions.

Llama 3.1 8B Instruct is a compact 8B dense model offering 128K context, making it one of the most capable small open models for long-document tasks at launch (July 2024). Runs comfortably on consumer GPUs with 8–10 GB VRAM at Q4. Strong for structured output, RAG pipelines, and agentic tasks where cost matters. Weaker on multi-step reasoning and math compared to Phi-3 Medium or Gemma 2 9B. The r/LocalLLaMA community favors it for server-side deployments needing high throughput at low cost.

Llama 3.2 11B Vision Instruct is a 11B multimodal model pairing a vision encoder (based on a ViT adapter) with the Llama 3.1 8B language backbone, released September 2024. It handles image+text inputs for tasks like VQA, chart reading, and image captioning with a 128K token context. Quality is competitive with LLaVA-1.6 13B and approaches InternVL2-8B; weaker than GPT-4o-mini on complex visual reasoning. Runs on a single 16–24 GB GPU. A practical choice for open-weight multimodal RAG pipelines.

Llama 3.2 1B Instruct is Meta's smallest text-only model from the September 2024 Llama 3.2 release, targeting on-device and edge inference. At 1B parameters it fits in ~1 GB RAM at Q4 and can run on mobile CPUs. Performance is limited — useful for simple classification, summarization stubs, and intent detection, but struggles with multi-step reasoning. Primarily valuable as a distillation target or an always-on local assistant; not competitive with Phi-3 Mini or Gemma 2 2B on quality benchmarks.

Llama 3.2 3B Instruct is Meta's small text model from September 2024, offering noticeably better reasoning than the 1B sibling while still targeting edge and mobile deployment. Uses the same 128K context architecture as the larger 3.2 models. Community benchmarks show it trading blows with Phi-3 Mini 3.8B on instruction following while being more context-capable. Fits in ~2 GB at Q4; a solid default for on-device chat applications where Phi-4 Mini is too large.

Llama 3.2 90B Vision Instruct is Meta's large open multimodal model, pairing a vision encoder with the Llama 3.1 70B backbone for high-quality image understanding. Released September 2024; at 90B total parameters it requires 2–4 GPUs for efficient inference. Tops open-weight vision leaderboards at release, competitive with Claude 3 Haiku on image tasks. Context window is 128K with the same caveats as other 3.x models. The 11B vision model covers most practical use cases more economically; the 90B shines on detailed document analysis and complex visual question answering.

Llama 3.3 70B Instruct is Meta's late-2024 refresh of the 70B dense transformer, delivering performance on par with Llama 3.1 405B on many benchmarks at a fraction of the compute cost. It features a 128K token context window, grouped-query attention, and strong instruction-following with improved multilingual coverage over 3.1. The r/LocalLLaMA community widely considers it the best weight-efficient open model of its generation — excellent for coding, reasoning, and chat at Q4 quant on a single 48 GB GPU. Chief weakness is the 128K context becoming slow and degrading in quality beyond ~32K in practice.

Llama 4 Maverick is a Mixture-of-Experts model with 17B active parameters out of 400B total, using 128 experts per layer, released April 2025. It supports natively multimodal input (text + images) and a 1M token context window. On public benchmarks it rivals GPT-4o and Gemini 1.5 Pro while activating only 17B parameters per token, yielding high throughput at inference. The r/LocalLLaMA community noted early concerns about benchmark overfitting and found real-world chat quality slightly trailing Llama 3.3 70B for pure-text tasks; however, vision and long-context performance is strong. Requires MoE-aware serving (e.g., vLLM with expert parallelism) for full efficiency.

12B natively multimodal safety classifier (pruned from Llama 4 Scout) covering 14 MLCommons hazard categories including violent crimes, child exploitation, self-harm, and code interpreter abuse. Supports multi-image prompt and response classification; integrated into the Llama Moderations API.

Llama 2 70B is Meta's July 2023 dense 70B base model, trained on 2T tokens with a 4K context window and GQA for efficient inference. It was the dominant open-weight model through late 2023 and spawned a large fine-tune ecosystem (WizardLM, Airoboros, etc.). Today it is substantially outperformed by Llama 3 8B on most benchmarks, making it primarily of historical interest. Its 4K context is a significant limitation for modern use cases; users on older workflows who haven't migrated should upgrade to the Llama 3.x series.

Llama 3 8B is Meta's April 2024 dense base model, the predecessor to the 3.1 series, with an 8K native context window and strong multilingual tokenization via a 128K vocabulary. It outperformed Llama 2 13B and Mistral 7B on most benchmarks at release. This is the raw base (non-instruct) checkpoint, best suited for further fine-tuning rather than direct chat deployment. Superseded by Llama 3.1 8B Instruct for most downstream use but remains a popular fine-tuning starting point due to its clean pretraining distribution.

Phi-3 Medium 128K Instruct is Microsoft's 14B dense model from the May 2024 Phi-3 release, extending the 4K context variant with LongRoPE to 128K tokens. At 14B it offers substantially better reasoning than Phi-3 Mini while remaining runnable on 1×24 GB GPU at Q4. Benchmarks place it near Llama 3 8B and above on several tasks despite being larger, due to the high-quality synthetic training data. However, long-context quality degrades significantly beyond 32K in practice, and for the 14B size class, Gemma 2 9B IT offers a strong alternative with better real-world benchmarks.

Phi-3 Medium 4K Instruct is the base 4K-context version of Microsoft's 14B Phi-3 Medium model released May 2024. Within its 4K window it achieves very high quality relative to parameter count, matching or beating Llama 3 8B Instruct on coding and STEM reasoning. The 4K limit is a significant constraint for long document tasks, making the 128K variant preferable when VRAM allows. Community consensus: excellent for structured, short-context tasks (code generation, classification, function calling) but use the 128K variant or a different model for anything requiring longer context.

Phi-3 Mini 128K Instruct is the long-context 3.8B variant of Microsoft's first Phi-3 Mini (April 2024), using LongRoPE to extend context to 128K tokens. The long context comes with quality trade-offs: Artificial Analysis and community tests show performance degrading substantially beyond 16–32K tokens. Within short contexts it is an impressive 3.8B model trained on 3.3T tokens of high-quality synthetic data. Superseded by Phi-3.5 Mini and then Phi-4 Mini but still widely deployed in edge settings for its stability and broad framework support (ONNX, llama.cpp, etc.).

Phi-3 Mini 4K Instruct is the original 3.8B Phi-3 Mini (April 2024) with a standard 4K context window, representing Microsoft's bet that data quality beats model scale. It outperformed Llama 2 7B and Mistral 7B on academic benchmarks at launch and runs efficiently on mobile hardware. The 4K context limits its utility for document-heavy tasks. Now superseded by Phi-3.5 Mini and Phi-4 Mini, but remains a reference point for 'small but capable' model design. Widely supported by Ollama, llama.cpp, and Hugging Face transformers.

Phi-3 Small 128K Instruct is Microsoft's 7B dense model from May 2024, sitting between Phi-3 Mini and Phi-3 Medium. It uses a unique tokenizer with multilingual vocabulary and achieves strong multilingual and reasoning performance at the 7B tier. Like other Phi-3 long-context variants, the 128K context degrades in practice beyond 32K. Community reception was mixed — at 7B it competes with Mistral 7B and Llama 3 8B but without a clear win; most users gravitated toward the more battle-tested competitors. Stronger multilingual performance is its main differentiator.

Phi-3 Small 8K Instruct is the 7B, 8K-context variant of Phi-3 Small (May 2024), optimized for constrained inference environments that need more than Mini's 3.8B but cannot accommodate Medium's 14B. Within the 8K window it achieves near-identical quality to the 128K variant with faster inference. Good for function calling, structured extraction, and multilingual chat. The 8K limit makes it less suitable for long-document tasks than similarly-sized competitors like Llama 3.1 8B. Paired with Phi-3 Medium for Microsoft's Azure AI Studio offerings at launch.

Phi-3.5 Mini is Microsoft's August 2024 3.8B dense model, a refresh of Phi-3 Mini with improved multilingual capability (supports 20+ languages), better instruction following, and retained 128K context via LongRoPE. It significantly narrows the gap to larger models on reasoning benchmarks compared to Phi-3 Mini. Runs on CPU or mobile with 4-bit quantization. The r/LocalLLaMA community considers it one of the best sub-4B models for its release date; Phi-4 Mini has since superseded it but Phi-3.5 Mini remains widely deployed due to its broad library support.

Phi-3.5 Vision Instruct is Microsoft's August 2024 4.2B multimodal model combining a CLIP ViT-L/14 vision encoder with the Phi-3.5 Mini language core. Supports multi-image input (up to 32 images in a single context), a 128K token window, and strong performance on document VQA and chart reading tasks relative to its size. The multi-image capability is its key differentiator over single-image models. Community testing found it occasionally struggles with spatial reasoning and complex scene descriptions; best suited for structured visual data (forms, tables, charts).

Phi-4 Mini Flash Reasoning is Microsoft's 2025 reasoning-specialized variant of Phi-4 Mini, fine-tuned with reinforcement learning (likely GRPO or similar) to produce chain-of-thought reasoning traces before answering. Targets math competition and STEM reasoning tasks. On AIME and MATH benchmarks it punches well above its 3.8B weight class, approaching or matching much larger non-reasoning models. A practical choice for constrained environments needing structured multi-step reasoning. Inference is slower per token due to longer thinking chains; not ideal for latency-sensitive applications.

Phi-4 Mini Instruct is Microsoft's February 2025 3.8B dense model, succeeding Phi-3.5 Mini with significantly improved reasoning and coding performance. Trained on a high-quality synthetic and curated dataset following Phi-4 Full's data recipe. On MMLU, HumanEval, and math benchmarks it outperforms Llama 3.2 3B and Gemma 2 2B, sometimes matching 7–8B models. Context window is 128K. Designed for on-device and edge inference; popular in agentic pipelines where a capable but cheap model handles tool routing. Weakness: limited world knowledge coverage due to small scale.

Phi-4 Multimodal Instruct is Microsoft's February 2025 5.6B multimodal model supporting text, image, audio, and video inputs in a unified architecture. It achieves top results on OCR, chart understanding, and speech benchmarks among sub-10B models, and is notable for supporting audio-to-text reasoning without a separate ASR step. The mixture-of-modalities design uses modality-specific encoders feeding a shared Phi-4 Mini language core. Community reception is positive for its breadth, though pure-text quality is slightly below Phi-4 Mini Full due to the multimodal training tradeoffs.

TRELLIS (Microsoft Research) is a large-scale 3D asset generation model that outputs Structured Latent (SLAT) representations—combining Gaussian splats, radiance fields, and mesh geometry—from a single image or text prompt. Input is an image or text description; output is a unified 3D asset exportable as mesh, NeRF, or 3D Gaussian Splatting representation. It surpasses prior single-image 3D reconstruction methods on GSO and OmniObject3D benchmarks and uniquely supports multiple output geometry formats from one inference pass.

MiniMax-M2.5 is a 230B MoE model (10B active) released open-weight under Apache 2.0. It achieves 80.2% on SWE-bench Verified, 51.3% on Multi-SWE-bench, and 76.3% on BrowseComp — placing it near the Claude Opus tier on coding tasks. Inference throughput is ~100 tokens/second natively, roughly twice that of other frontier-class models. Costs half as much as M2.7 per token on API providers.

MiniMax-M2.7 is a 230B MoE model (10B active) with self-evolving capability: the model ran 100+ autonomous rounds of 'analyze failures → modify scaffold → evaluate → revert or keep', achieving a reported 30% improvement on internal evals. It is proprietary (not open-weight), unlike earlier M2 and M2.5 releases. It scores 56.22% on SWE-Pro multilingual and 55.6% on VIBE-Pro repo-level coding, competitive with Opus 4.6 and GPT-5.3-Codex on those benchmarks.

Codestral 22B is Mistral's first dedicated code-generation model, a 22B dense transformer fluent in 80+ programming languages including Python, Java, C/C++, JavaScript, Bash, and Fortran. Released May 2024 under the Mistral AI Non-Production License (commercial use requires a separate agreement), it set state-of-the-art results among open code models at launch and is widely used in code-completion integrations. Community adoption is strong but the non-commercial default license has limited some downstream use cases relative to Apache-licensed alternatives.

Devstral 2 is a 123B MoE transformer with 88 layers and a 256K context window, released December 2025 in FP8 and optimized for software-engineering agentic tasks including multi-file codebase editing, tool use, and integration with OpenHands and SWE-Agent scaffolds. It achieves 46.8% on SWE-Bench Verified, surpassing prior open-source state-of-the-art by over 6 percentage points. Community discussion on HuggingFace noted an apparent discrepancy where system prompts mention vision capabilities but no vision encoder weights are included in the release.

Magistral Small (2506) is a 24B dense transformer reasoning model built on Mistral Small 3.1 (2503) with reasoning capabilities added via SFT on Magistral Medium traces followed by RL, released June 2025 under Apache 2.0. It supports a 128K context, produces extended chain-of-thought traces before answering, and covers 25+ languages. It scored 70.7% on AIME 2024 (83.3% with majority voting @64) and is deployable on a single RTX 4090 or 32GB Mac, making it a competitive open small reasoning model in the DeepSeek-R1-Distill class.

Mamba Codestral 7B is a 7B code-generation model built on the Mamba2 state-space architecture rather than a standard transformer, released July 2024 under Apache 2.0. Unlike transformers, Mamba2 offers linear-time sequence processing and supports theoretically unlimited context length (demonstrated up to 256K tokens). It scored 75.0% on HumanEval, outperforming CodeGemma-1.1 7B (61%) and DeepSeek v1.5 7B (66%); Artificial Analysis highlighted it as one of the first serious Mamba-based code models, with favorable throughput at long contexts.

Mathstral 7B is a 7B dense transformer fine-tuned from Mistral 7B specifically for mathematical and STEM reasoning, released July 2024 under Apache 2.0. It uses a 32K context window and achieves 56.6% on MATH and 63.5% on MMLU, improving to 68.4% on MATH with majority voting (64 samples) and 74.6% with a strong reward model. Community reception was positive as a lightweight specialist; Artificial Analysis noted it as one of the first open 7B models to take math fine-tuning seriously using inference-time scaling techniques.

Ministral 3 14B Instruct (2512) is a 14B multimodal model—13.5B language parameters plus a 0.4B vision encoder—released December 2025 under Apache 2.0. It supports up to 10 images per prompt, a 262K token context window, native function calling, and multilingual text across 11 languages, deployable on a single H200 GPU in FP8 (~24 GB VRAM). It is positioned as an efficient edge-and-datacenter vision-capable model in the Mistral 3 family, sitting below Mistral Large 3 in capability but sharing the multimodal architecture.

Mistral Large 2 (Mistral-Large-Instruct-2407) is a dense 123B-parameter transformer released July 2024 with a 128K context window, designed for single-node inference. It significantly improved on the original Mistral Large in code generation, mathematics, reasoning, and multilingual support across 80+ coding languages and a dozen natural languages. Benchmarks at release showed competitiveness with GPT-4o, Claude 3 Opus, and Llama 3 405B, and it was broadly praised as the best open-weight large model at the time of launch.

Mistral 7B Instruct v0.1 is the instruction-tuned version of Mistral's landmark 7B dense decoder-only transformer, released October 2023. The architecture features Grouped Query Attention, Sliding Window Attention (4K window), and a Byte-fallback BPE tokenizer with an 8K context window. It outperformed all 7B chat models on MT-Bench at release and demonstrated that a 7B model could match 13B-class performance, sparking widespread community adoption and a rich ecosystem of fine-tunes.

Mistral 7B Instruct v0.3 is a revised instruction fine-tune of the Mistral 7B v0.3 base, the main upgrade being an extended 32,768-token vocabulary (up from the original) with a v3 tokenizer that adds native function-calling tokens (TOOL_CALLS, AVAILABLE_TOOLS, TOOL_RESULTS). Architecture is otherwise identical to v0.1: dense 7B decoder-only with GQA, SWA, and an 8K context. It remains one of the most widely quantized and deployed 7B models in the open-source ecosystem.

The original Mistral Large (February 2024) was Mistral's first flagship closed-access API model, a dense transformer of undisclosed size positioned to compete with GPT-4 class models. It introduced strong multilingual reasoning, native function calling, and JSON output for enterprise workflows. Mistral superseded it with Mistral Large 2 in July 2024, which 'vastly outperformed' this version; the original is largely legacy at this point and rarely benchmarked in isolation.

Mistral Large 3 is a granular Mixture-of-Experts multimodal model with 675B total parameters and 41B active parameters (39B LM + 2.5B vision encoder), released December 2025. It supports a 256K context window, native function calling, JSON output, and multilingual text plus image input across 11+ languages. On a single node it is deployable in FP8 (B200/H200) or NVFP4 (H100/A100). Community reception is positive for its agentic capabilities and vision integration, though it is noted as adequate rather than class-leading on hard math competition tasks.

Mistral Medium 3 is a dense, transformer-based frontier model positioned between Small and Large in Mistral's lineup, released mid-2025 with parameter count undisclosed by Mistral. It targets enterprise workloads—coding, STEM reasoning, and multimodal understanding—at significantly lower cost (claimed up to 8x cheaper than comparable closed alternatives). Reviewers highlight strong coding and STEM benchmark results that rival much larger competitors, making it a strong cost-efficiency choice, though exact architectural details remain proprietary.

Mistral Nemotron is a large language model co-developed by Mistral AI and NVIDIA, released June 2025 on NVIDIA NIM as part of the Nemotron Coalition partnership. Built on a modified Transformer architecture and NVIDIA-optimized for TensorRT-LLM and vLLM on Hopper-class GPUs, it supports a 128K context window and is tuned for agentic workflows including tool calling. It represents the first tangible output of the Mistral-NVIDIA co-development agreement announced in 2025; exact parameter count is not publicly disclosed.

Mistral Small 3 (2501) is a 24B dense transformer with 40 layers, GQA with 8 KV heads, a 32K context window, and SwiGLU activation, released January 2025 under Apache 2.0—the first Mistral Small with a fully permissive commercial license. It reaches performance on par with Llama 3.3 70B at over 3x faster inference on the same hardware, with strong instruction-following improvements over the prior 2409 version. Community response is enthusiastic for its open license and efficiency; some users note weakened pop-culture knowledge compared to 2409, but instruction compliance is markedly better.

Mistral Small 3.1 is a 24B dense transformer released March 2025, adding multimodal vision understanding and extending context to 128K tokens over the preceding Mistral Small 3 (2501). It uses a Tekken tokenizer with 131K vocabulary and supports text plus image inputs across 11+ languages. The model runs on a single RTX 4090 or 32GB Mac and is well-regarded by the community as a capable lightweight multimodal option suitable for on-device and enterprise use; the October 2023 training cutoff has been flagged as a limitation for freshness-sensitive tasks.

Mistral Small 4 is a 119B-parameter MoE model with 128 experts and 6.5B active parameters, released March 2026 with a 256K context window and native vision support. Uniquely, it unifies instruct, reasoning (Magistral-style), and code-agent (Devstral-style) capabilities into one model, allowing dynamic adjustment of reasoning effort at inference time. It achieves a 40% reduction in end-to-end latency versus Mistral Small 3 in latency-optimized configurations and matches or beats GPT-OSS 120B on reasoning benchmarks while producing significantly shorter outputs.

Mixtral 8x22B Instruct is a sparse MoE model with 141B total parameters and 39B active parameters per token (8 experts per layer, top-2 routing), released April 2024. It was the most capable open-weight model at launch, featuring a 64K context window, native function calling, and multilingual support, while running faster than any dense 70B model due to sparse activation. The AI community widely celebrated it as a breakthrough in open-source cost-performance, and it held the top open-source position until Llama 3 405B arrived.

Mixtral 8x7B Instruct is a sparse Mixture-of-Experts decoder-only model with 46.7B total parameters and 12.9B active parameters per token (8 experts, top-2 routing), released December 2023. It runs at the inference cost of a ~13B model while outperforming Llama 2 70B on most benchmarks with 6x faster inference. Community reception was extremely positive, establishing MoE as the dominant open-source architecture for efficiency; the instruction-tuned variant scored top results on MT-Bench among open models at release.

Boltz-2 (MIT/Recursion) is an open-weight biomolecular structure prediction model that extends Boltz-1's AlphaFold3-style architecture to jointly predict protein-ligand, protein-nucleic-acid, and multi-chain complexes while also estimating binding affinities. Input is sequences plus optional SMILES for small molecules; output is all-atom complex coordinates with confidence scores and a predicted binding affinity value. It matches or exceeds AlphaFold3 accuracy on the PoseBusters benchmark while being fully open-source.

DiffDock (MIT CSAIL) is a diffusion generative model for molecular docking that treats docking as a generative problem over ligand poses rather than a scoring/search problem. Input is a protein structure (PDB) and a ligand (SDF/SMILES); output is a ranked set of predicted bound poses with confidence scores. On the PoseBusters benchmark it achieves significantly higher success rates than AutoDock Vina and traditional docking methods, especially for flexible and novel ligands.

Kimi-K2-5 appears to be an alternate ID slug for the Kimi K2.5 model (moonshotai/Kimi-K2.5 on HuggingFace). Kimi-K2.5 is a natively multimodal 1T MoE model (32B active) built via continual pretraining on ~15T mixed visual and text tokens atop Kimi-K2-Base, adding vision understanding via MoonViT (400M parameters). Supports instant and thinking modes, 256K context window.

Kimi-K2-Instruct is the instruction-following variant of the Kimi-K2 1T MoE (32B active) model, released June 2025. It achieves 65.8% pass@1 on SWE-bench Verified and 47.3% on SWE-bench Multilingual under single-attempt, no-test-time-compute conditions. The architecture uses 384 total experts, 8 selected per token plus 1 shared expert, across 61 layers with a 7,168 attention hidden dimension. Trained on 15.5T tokens using the Muon optimizer.

Kimi-K2-Instruct-0905 is a September 2025 updated checkpoint of Kimi-K2-Instruct, retaining the 1T MoE (32B active) architecture. Model weights are stored in block-fp8 format for inference efficiency. The recommended temperature is 0.6; the update focuses on improved tool-calling reliability and agentic task performance. Quantized community GGUF versions are available from bartowski and unsloth.

Kimi-K2-Thinking is a 1T-parameter MoE model (32B active) released by Moonshot AI as a thinking/reasoning agent that reasons step-by-step while invoking tools across 200–300 sequential calls. It sets state-of-the-art on Humanity's Last Exam (HLE with tools: 44.9%), BrowseComp (60.2%), SWE-bench Verified (71.3%), and AIME 2025 (99.1% with Python). The checkpoint is distributed in native INT4 quantization, with a 256K context window.

BEVFormer is a transformer-based autonomous-driving perception model that aggregates multi-camera video into a unified Bird's-Eye-View (BEV) representation using spatial and temporal cross-attention. Input is synchronized multi-camera images (typically 6 surround cameras); output is a BEV feature map used for 3D object detection and map segmentation without LiDAR. It set state-of-the-art results on nuScenes when published and became the dominant camera-only BEV detection baseline adopted by the AV research community.

CorrDiff is NVIDIA's corrective diffusion model for statistical downscaling of global weather forecasts to km-scale regional resolution. Input is a coarse (25 km) ERA5 or GFS atmospheric field; output is a high-resolution (2 km) ensemble of physically consistent precipitation, wind, and temperature fields. By learning residual corrections via diffusion rather than direct super-resolution, CorrDiff preserves large-scale structure while generating realistic fine-scale spatial patterns not present in the coarse input.

NVIDIA cuOpt is a GPU-accelerated combinatorial optimization microservice for vehicle routing, supply-chain scheduling, and last-mile logistics. Input is a routing problem specification (distance matrix, vehicle capacities, time windows, constraints) via REST API or Python SDK; output is an optimized route plan with assignment and sequencing decisions. It leverages GPU parallelism to solve NP-hard VRP variants orders of magnitude faster than CPU-bound OR-Tools or commercial solvers, targeting real-time re-routing use cases.

1024-dimensional QA retrieval embedding model (E5-Large-Unsupervised fine-tune, 24 layers) with a 512-token context window. An earlier generation of NVIDIA's NeMo Retriever embedding series, intended for English QA and RAG use cases; succeeded by nv-embedqa-e5-v5.

FourCastNet is NVIDIA's Fourier Neural Operator-based global weather forecasting model trained on ERA5 reanalysis data at 0.25-degree resolution. Input is a set of atmospheric state variables (wind, temperature, humidity, geopotential) at one or two time steps; output is a 6-hour forecast rolled autoregressively to multi-week lead times. It produces 10-day forecasts 45,000x faster than traditional NWP models and achieves accuracy competitive with ECMWF's operational IFS for many key variables.

GenMol is NVIDIA's discrete diffusion model for de novo small-molecule generation and hit-to-lead optimization, operating in SELFIES token space to guarantee chemical validity. Input is a seed SELFIES string or a target property profile; output is a set of novel SELFIES-encoded molecules with predicted drug-likeness and synthesizability scores. Compared to SMILES-based autoregressive models, the discrete diffusion approach enables non-sequential edits and better exploration of chemical space around a scaffold.

Fine-tuned GLiNER span-extraction model for PII and PHI detection, achieving 92% recall and 64% F1 across entity types including names, email addresses, phone numbers, and social security numbers. Designed for privacy-preserving AI pipelines and data anonymisation workflows.

NVIDIA Ising Calibration is a 1.35B-parameter mixture-of-experts language model (3B active parameters) specialized for mapping combinatorial optimization problems described in natural language or structured formats into Ising/QUBO formulations ready for quantum annealers or GPU Ising solvers. Input is a problem description (text or JSON); output is a validated QUBO matrix with variable mappings and penalty terms. It bridges the gap between problem specification and quantum-ready formulation, reducing the expert effort required to onboard new optimization problems.

8B multilingual content safety guard (LoRA on Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct, Nemotron-Safety-Guard-Dataset-v3) with strong zero-shot generalisation to 20+ languages and explicit support for English, Spanish, Mandarin, German, French, Hindi, Japanese, Arabic, and Thai. Classifies 9 harm categories.

8B LoRA-tuned content safety classifier (Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct base) trained on the Aegis Content Safety Dataset (~35K annotated samples). Classifies user prompts and LLM responses as safe or unsafe across NVIDIA's content safety taxonomy; achieves 94% product-safety accuracy.

8B instruction-tuned guardrail model (Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct base) that keeps AI conversations within user-defined approved topic boundaries. Fine-tuned on synthetic data for consistent topic enforcement across multi-turn dialogues; used in customer-service and compliance-sensitive agentic deployments.

Llama 3.1 Nemotron Nano 4B v1.1 is a 4B dense transformer derived from Llama 3.1 8B via NVIDIA's Minitron width-pruning and knowledge-distillation compression technique, then post-trained for math, code, reasoning, and tool calling with RPO-based RL. It supports a 128K context window and runs on a single RTX GPU or consumer device. As a compressed derivative of an 8B model, it offers a smaller memory footprint than Nano 8B while maintaining competitive performance on reasoning benchmarks for its size class.

Llama 3.1 Nemotron Nano 8B v1 is an 8B dense decoder-only transformer post-trained from Meta Llama 3.1 8B Instruct with a focus on reasoning, human preference alignment, RAG, and tool calling. Training used SFT on math, code, and reasoning tasks followed by RLOO and Online RPO RL stages; it supports a 128K context window and runs on a single consumer RTX GPU. It is NVIDIA's entry-level reasoning model in the Nemotron Nano family, balancing on-device deployability with competitive reasoning scores at the 8B scale.

Llama 3.1 Nemotron Nano VL 8B v1 is an 8B vision-language model pairing a C-RADIOv2-H vision encoder with a Llama 3.1 8B Instruct language backbone, released June 2025 and optimized for document intelligence tasks. It supports image and text inputs up to a 16K combined context, handling multi-tile images up to 2048×1536 at a 12-tile 512×512-per-tile layout, with strong OCR, document Q&A, and image summarization capabilities (English only). Hardware target is NVIDIA H100 SXM via TensorRT-LLM; it is positioned as an edge-deployable document-understanding VLM.

8B multilingual content-safety moderator (Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct base) officially supporting 9 languages (English, Spanish, Mandarin, German, French, Hindi, Japanese, Arabic, Thai) and detecting 9 unsafe content categories. Returns structured JSON with User Safety, Response Safety, and Safety Categories fields.

Llama 3.1 Nemotron Ultra 253B v1 is a 253B dense decoder-only transformer derived from Meta Llama 3.1 405B Instruct via Neural Architecture Search (NAS), released April 2025. NAS introduces skip-attention layers, variable FFN widths, and FFN fusion, reducing the model to 253B parameters with significantly improved inference efficiency while fitting on a single 8xH100 node with 128K context. Post-training used multi-stage SFT and GRPO-based RL across math, code, reasoning, chat, and tool calling; it meets or exceeds DeepSeek-R1 reasoning performance with higher throughput.

Multimodal 1B VLM embedding model combining Llama 3.2 1B with SigLIP2 400M (Eagle 2 architecture), producing 2048-dimensional embeddings for text, images, and combined inputs. First-generation multimodal NeMo Retriever model; succeeded by llama-nemotron-embed-vl-1b-v2.

Compact 300M-class text embedding model (9-layer transformer, 2048-dim, Matryoshka-configurable to 384–2048) distilled from llama-3.2-nv-embedqa-1b-v1. Multilingual across 26 languages with 8192-token document support; designed for edge and resource-constrained deployments.

Improved 300M-class multilingual text embedding model (9-layer, 2048-dim, Matryoshka 384–2048) with enhanced multilingual and cross-lingual accuracy over v1. Covers 26 languages and up to 8192-token documents; suitable for efficient on-device and latency-sensitive RAG.

~500M-parameter cross-encoder reranker distilled from the 1B NeMo Retriever reranker family, outputting passage-level relevance probability scores for QA retrieval. Designed as a lightweight alternative to 1B-class rerankers for latency-constrained multilingual RAG pipelines.

2048-dimensional multilingual QA retrieval embedding model fine-tuned from Llama 3.2 1B (16 layers). Pre-trained on 100M public samples, fine-tuned on 1M; covers 26 languages including Arabic, Chinese, Japanese, and major European languages. Compact size optimised for latency-sensitive deployments.

2048-dimensional multilingual embedding model (Llama 3.2 1B fine-tune) with Matryoshka Embeddings (configurable from 256 to 2048 dims) and support for long documents up to 8192 tokens. Evaluated on 26 languages; improved multilingual and cross-lingual accuracy over v1.

Cross-encoder reranker built on Llama 3.2 1B, fine-tuned for multilingual QA ranking across 26 languages. Compact 1B-class model suitable for latency-sensitive RAG pipelines; trained on ~800K public samples with multilingual and cross-lingual coverage.

Improved cross-encoder reranker on Llama 3.2 1B supporting long documents (up to 8192 tokens) and 26 languages; 3.5× smaller than nv-rerankqa-mistral-4b-v3 with competitive accuracy. Suited for multilingual RAG pipelines that need low memory footprint.

Llama 3.3 Nemotron Super 49B v1 is a 49B dense decoder-only transformer derived from Meta Llama 3.3 70B Instruct via NAS (skip-attention, variable FFN), enabling single-GPU (H100/H200) deployment with 128K context. Post-training applied SFT across math, code, science, and multi-turn chat, followed by RPO, RLVR, and iterative DPO for reasoning and alignment. It is a strong reasoning-capable chat model with agentic support (RAG, tool calling) in English and seven additional languages, offering a favorable accuracy-to-efficiency tradeoff for single-GPU inference.

Llama 3.3 Nemotron Super 49B v1.5 is a refined iteration of v1 sharing the same NAS-derived 49B architecture from Llama 3.3 70B Instruct, with further post-training improvements targeting reasoning, chat alignment, and agentic workflow quality. Internal benchmarks report MATH500 97.4%, AIME-2024 87.5%, AIME-2025 82.7%, GPQA 72.0%, and LiveCodeBench 73.6%, placing it among the top-performing 49B-class open models. It inherits the single-GPU (H100/H200) deployment profile and 128K context of v1.

Multilingual text embedding model in the Llama Nemotron NeMo Retriever family, supporting 8192-token documents and Matryoshka configurable dimensions; covers 26 languages. Optimised for use with llama-nemotron-rerank-1b-v2 to achieve high BEIR+TechQA accuracy with multilingual support.

2048-dimensional multimodal embedding model (~1.7B parameters) combining Llama 3.2 1B with a SigLIP2 400M vision encoder. Embeds text, images, or image+text combinations; supports visual documents (PDFs, charts, infographics) and achieves 71–73% Recall@5 on visual document retrieval benchmarks.

1B-parameter cross-encoder reranker paired with llama-nemotron-embed-1b-v2, providing relevance logit scores for multilingual QA across 26 languages with 8192-token document support. Part of NVIDIA's latest NeMo Retriever generation released in 2025.

~1.7B-parameter multimodal cross-encoder reranker (Llama 3.2 1B + SigLIP2 400M, Eagle VLM architecture) designed to rerank text and visual document candidates in multimodal RAG pipelines. Complements llama-nemotron-embed-vl-1b-v2 for visual document retrieval.

MAISI (Medical AI for Synthetic Imaging) is NVIDIA's latent diffusion model for generating synthetic 3D CT volumes conditioned on anatomical labels and body-region specifications. Input is a segmentation mask or body-part/pathology conditioning vector; output is a photorealistic 3D CT scan with corresponding ground-truth labels. It addresses the data scarcity and privacy constraints of medical imaging by generating arbitrarily large labeled training datasets for downstream segmentation and detection models.

Mistral NeMo Minitron 8B Base is an 8B dense transformer produced by applying NVIDIA's width-pruning compression to Mistral NeMo 12B and retraining with knowledge distillation on 380B tokens, released August 2024. The resulting architecture has 40 layers, 4096 embedding dimension, 11520 MLP intermediate dimension, GQA, and RoPE. It consistently outperforms similarly sized base models across nine benchmarks, achieves 1.2x throughput improvement over Mistral NeMo 12B on H100 80GB when served with TensorRT-LLM, and is positioned as NVIDIA's demonstration of efficient LLM compression via the Minitron approach.

MolMIM is NVIDIA's masked-image-modeling-inspired molecular encoder-decoder that learns compact latent representations of SMILES strings for property-guided optimization. Input is a SMILES molecule; output is a latent embedding that can be decoded back to SMILES or steered toward desired physicochemical properties via CMA-ES optimization in latent space. It is designed as a lightweight baseline for constrained molecular generation tasks where larger diffusion models are computationally prohibitive.

Specialised NeMo Guardrails model for detecting jailbreak and adversarial prompt-injection attempts designed to bypass LLM safety filters. Deployed as a NIM microservice for real-time agentic pipeline protection; complements content-safety and topic-control guardrails.

OCR and document-parsing model built on an 885M-parameter encoder-decoder (ViT-H/16 vision encoder + 256M language decoder) that converts complex PDFs into structured Markdown with bounding boxes and semantic reading order. Achieves near-perfect scores on the GOT benchmark at ~4–5 pages/second.

4B multimodal and multilingual content safety classifier fine-tuned by NVIDIA from Google Gemma-3-4B-it, supporting 9 languages and 128K context length. Can moderate both LLM inputs and responses; part of the latest Nemotron safety model series released in 2025.

Nemotron 3 Nano is a 31.6B-total/3.2B-active (approximately 3.5B active per sources) hybrid Mamba-Transformer MoE model sharing the architectural family of Nemotron 3 Super, released December 2025. It uses interleaved Mamba-2 and MoE layers with 128 experts (plus 1 shared) and 6 active per token alongside 6 attention layers, supports a 1M-token context window, and achieves up to 3.3x higher throughput than comparable models. It supports configurable reasoning traces for both agentic and conversational tasks and is fully open-weight.

Nemotron 3 Super is a 120B-total/12B-active hybrid Latent Mixture-of-Experts model interleaving Mamba-2, LatentMoE, and attention layers, released in 2025 with a 1-million-token context window. Pre-trained on 25 trillion tokens using NVFP4 on NVIDIA Blackwell, it incorporates Multi-Token Prediction layers for improved throughput and quality. It matches or exceeds GPT-OSS 120B on accuracy while delivering 11% higher throughput per B200 GPU and 2.2x higher throughput on standard 8K/16K workloads; designed for complex multi-agent agentic applications like software development and cybersecurity.

Nemotron 4 Mini Hindi 4B Instruct is a 4B dense transformer decoder fine-tuned for the Hindi-language domain, supporting Hindi, English, and Hinglish queries grounded in Indian cultural context. It shares the 3072-embedding/32-head GQA/RoPE/9216-MLP-dim architecture of Nemotron Mini 4B with a 4K context window, trained June–October 2024. It is one of very few openly released Hindi-specialized SLMs from a major infrastructure AI lab and is approved for commercial use under NVIDIA's license terms.

4B LLM-based content safety classifier with a 'bring your own policy' design: users define custom safety rules and the model adapts its classification and reasoning accordingly. Supports Reasoning On mode (explicit chain-of-thought) or Reasoning Off mode for fast standard classification; low memory footprint.

Nemotron Mini 4B Instruct is a 4B dense transformer decoder fine-tuned from the Minitron-4B-Base (itself pruned and distilled from Nemotron-4 15B), released September 2024. It uses a 3072-embedding, 32-head GQA architecture with RoPE, a 4K context window, and approximately 2 GB VRAM when quantized, making it suitable for on-device and edge deployment. Fine-tuned for roleplay, RAG, and function calling, it is NVIDIA's most compact instruct model and was open-sourced to enable rapid prototyping on consumer hardware.

Nemotron Nano 12B v2 VL is a 12B multimodal reasoning model pairing the RADIOv2.5/CRadioV2-H vision encoder with the Nemotron Nano v2 12B hybrid Mamba-Transformer language backbone, supporting image, video (MP4/MOV/WEBM), and text inputs with a 128K context. Trained with SFT across 270 datasets (27.7 TB total) covering document, chart, OCR, and video understanding, it achieves leading results on OCRBench v2 and averages approximately 74 across MMMU, MathVista, AI2D, OCRBench, ChartQA, DocVQA, and Video-MME. It is NVIDIA's primary compact vision-language reasoning model as of late 2025.

Nemotron-Parse 1.1: 885M-parameter document-parsing VLM (ViT-H/16 + 256M decoder) succeeding NemoRetriever-Parse-1.0, with improved OCR, table parsing, chart/diagram extraction, and structured Markdown output. Includes a token-compressed (TC) variant for higher-speed inference at ~5 pages/second.

Self-supervised vision foundation model trained with DINO+iBOT on NVIDIA's proprietary commercial dataset; available in 224-resolution (130M) and 518-resolution (700M) variants. Produces image embeddings for classification, segmentation, and detection; runs on Jetson and data-center GPUs.

4096-dimensional generalist text embedding model based on Mistral-7B-v0.1 with a latent-attention pooling layer, supporting up to 32k-token inputs. Ranked #1 on the MTEB leaderboard (56 tasks, score 59.36 on 15 retrieval tasks) at its May 2024 release. English-focused; not explicitly multilingual.

4096-dimensional code and text embedding model with 7.1B parameters, fine-tuned from NVIDIA's Mistral-7B retrieval model. Trained on documents up to 512 tokens with a theoretical attention span of ~131K tokens; excels at hybrid code-text retrieval tasks. Commercially licensed.

1024-dimensional QA-retrieval embedding model fine-tuned from E5-Large-Unsupervised with 24 transformer layers, max 512-token input. Optimised for production RAG pipelines; commercially licensed. Pairs well with nv-rerankqa-mistral-4b-v3 to reach ~75% Recall@5 on QA benchmarks.

Open-vocabulary zero-shot object detection model based on Grounding DINO, detecting objects in images from free-text descriptions without requiring class-specific training. Commercially licensed NVIDIA-packaged variant; supports single and multi-object detection via natural language queries.

4B-parameter (first 16 layers of Mistral-7B-v0.1) LoRA-fine-tuned cross-encoder reranker using list-wise InfoNCE loss and bi-directional attention. Achieves ~75% Recall@5 when paired with nv-embedqa-e5-v5 on QA benchmarks; commercially licensed, multilingual.

1024-dimensional multimodal CLIP-style embedding model (ViT-H/14) trained on 700M proprietary NVIDIA images for joint image-text retrieval. Supports zero-shot image classification, multimodal search, and downstream computer vision tasks; commercially licensed enterprise alternative to OpenAI CLIP.

NVIDIA Nemotron Nano 9B v2 is a 9B hybrid Mamba-2/MLP/Transformer model trained from scratch (not a fine-tune), using predominantly Mamba-2 and MLP layers with only four attention layers, released September 2025 after training on 20+ trillion tokens. It supports a 131K context window and is designed as a unified reasoning and non-reasoning model; compared to Qwen3-8B it achieves on-par or better reasoning accuracy while reaching up to 6x higher inference throughput in reasoning mode. The technical report (arXiv 2508.14444) documents the hybrid architecture in detail.

NVIDIA TAO Toolkit pre-trained optical character detection model (OCDNet) that localises text regions in images. Serves as a detection backbone for downstream OCR pipelines; designed for integration with NVIDIA's computer vision and document AI workflows.

4B-parameter cross-encoder reranker (first 16 layers of Mistral-7B-v0.1 LoRA-tuned) trained with Binary CrossEntropy loss on 400K public QA pairs. Earlier generation of the Mistral-based NVIDIA reranker series; max 512 tokens per passage; superseded by nv-rerankqa-mistral-4b-v3.

EfficientDet + DINO-FAN_base CNN model fine-tuned to detect 100 specific retail product categories from video at 416×416 or 960×544 resolution. Part of NVIDIA's TAO Toolkit for retail analytics; optimised for on-shelf detection and inventory management use cases.

4B neural machine translation model pruned and distilled from Mistral-NeMo-Minitron-8B-Base, supporting bidirectional translation across 12 languages and dialects (English, German, French, Spanish ES/US, Portuguese BR, Russian, Chinese ZH-CN/TW, Japanese, Korean, Arabic) with 8K context. Trained June–August 2025; outperforms EuroLLM-9B-Instruct on FLORES and WMT24.

SparseDrive is NVIDIA's end-to-end autonomous driving model that jointly performs sparse 3D perception and motion planning in a unified transformer architecture without intermediate dense representations. Input is multi-camera images and ego-vehicle state; output is simultaneous 3D object detection, trajectory prediction for surrounding agents, and planned ego trajectory. By avoiding BEV grid construction it reduces memory and compute while maintaining or improving planning quality compared to modular pipelines.

StreamPETR is a streaming 3D object detection framework for autonomous driving that propagates object queries temporally across frames via memory-efficient propagation rather than full BEV construction. Input is multi-camera images from consecutive frames; output is 3D bounding boxes with class labels and velocities. It achieves accuracy superior to BEVFormer on nuScenes with significantly lower latency, making it practical for real-time on-vehicle inference.

USDCode is NVIDIA's code-generation model fine-tuned to write OpenUSD (Universal Scene Description) Python API calls and scene-description schemas for 3D asset and simulation workflows. Input is a natural-language description of a 3D scene operation or asset modification; output is executable USD Python code using the Pixar/NVIDIA USD API. It accelerates adoption of USD in production pipelines by bridging the gap between artist intent and the verbose USD programming model.

USDSearch is NVIDIA's semantic search and retrieval system for 3D assets described in OpenUSD format, embedding asset metadata and scene-graph structure for similarity retrieval. Input is a text query or a reference USD asset; output is a ranked list of matching 3D assets from a USD-indexed repository. It enables natural-language-driven asset discovery in large 3D content libraries (e.g., Omniverse digital twins) without requiring manual tagging or exact filename matching.

NVIDIA's Visual Language Intelligence model family (VILA/NVILA) for image and video question-answering, multi-image reasoning, and in-context visual learning; VILA-1.5 achieves 78.2% TextVQA. Optimised for deployment on Jetson Orin and data-centre GPUs via TensorRT-LLM; now part of Cosmos Nemotron as of January 2025.

VISTA3D is NVIDIA's universal 3D medical image segmentation model trained on 11,454 volumes spanning 127 classes across CT and MRI modalities. Input is a 3D volumetric scan with optional point or class prompts; output is per-voxel segmentation masks for any of the supported anatomical structures. It unifies class-prompted (everything) and interactive (click-guided) segmentation in a single model, outperforming prior task-specific models on the TotalSegmentator benchmark while requiring no fine-tuning for new organs.

Visual ChangeNet is NVIDIA's change-detection transformer for geospatial and industrial inspection use cases, identifying semantically meaningful differences between bi-temporal image pairs. Input is two registered images (satellite, aerial, or inspection imagery) from different time points; output is a pixel-level change map with class labels for added, removed, or modified structures. It outperforms prior change-detection models on LEVIR-CD and WHU-CD benchmarks by leveraging a Siamese attention backbone with explicit change-aware attention modules.

GPT-OSS 120B is OpenAI's first fully open-weight model release since GPT-2, a sparse MoE transformer with 117B total parameters and 5.1B active parameters per token (128 experts, top-4 routing, 36 layers), released August 2025 under Apache 2.0. It uses alternating dense and locally banded sparse attention with grouped multi-query attention (group size 8), RoPE positional encoding, a 128K context window, and was trained on a mostly English STEM/coding dataset using o200k_harmony tokenizer with RL informed by o3 and other frontier systems. It achieves near-parity with OpenAI o4-mini on core reasoning benchmarks while fitting on a single 80GB GPU (H100 or AMD MI300X).

GPT-OSS 20B is the smaller of OpenAI's open-weight pair, a sparse MoE transformer with 21B total parameters and 3.6B active parameters per token, released alongside GPT-OSS 120B in August 2025 under Apache 2.0. It shares the same MoE/attention/RoPE architecture as the 120B sibling but is designed for lower-latency and on-device or edge use cases, runnable with as little as 16GB of memory. Benchmarked against OpenAI's own o3-mini, it delivers comparable results on common reasoning and coding tasks and is regarded as a strong small open-weight reasoning model for local deployment.

OpenFold 2 is Columbia University's fully open-source reimplementation and extension of AlphaFold2 with trainable weights, supporting fine-tuning on custom datasets and novel distillation training regimes. Input and output match AlphaFold2 (sequence + MSA → all-atom coordinates). Unlike DeepMind's release which ships only inference weights, OpenFold exposes the full training pipeline, making it the standard base for academic research that requires retraining or domain adaptation of structure prediction models.

OpenFold 3 is the open-source reimplementation of AlphaFold3's Evoformer-plus-diffusion architecture, extending coverage from proteins to DNA, RNA, and small-molecule ligands in a unified model. Input is sequences and chemical identities of all chain types; output is all-atom complex structure with confidence metrics. It provides the training code and weights that AlphaFold3's official release withheld, enabling academic reproduction and custom training of full biomolecular complex prediction.

7B European multilingual LLM pre-trained from scratch on 4 trillion tokens covering all 24 official EU languages (~50% non-English data, data cutoff September 2023). Instruction-tuned on all 24 EU languages with a multilingual tokeniser for energy-efficient operation; developed by the OpenGPT-X consortium (Fraunhofer IAIS et al.).

Qwen2-7B-Instruct is a 7B instruction-tuned model from the Qwen2 generation, preceding the Qwen2.5 series. It uses GQA attention, SwiGLU, RMSNorm, and supports a 128K token context window. The Qwen2 generation introduced substantially improved multilingual coverage (29 languages) and coding capability over Qwen1.5. Community benchmarks show it competitive with Mistral-7B and Llama-3-8B on general tasks.

Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct is a 7B general-purpose instruction model from Alibaba's Qwen team using RoPE, SwiGLU, RMSNorm, and GQA architecture with up to 128K token context via YaRN. It serves as the base for Qwen2.5-Coder-7B and several DeepSeek distillation variants. Community users report it as a strong baseline for local deployment, efficient for its size class on reasoning and instruction following tasks.

Qwen2.5-Coder-32B-Instruct is a 32B instruction-tuned coding model from the Qwen2.5-Coder family, trained on 5.5T tokens of source code, synthetic data, and grounded text-code pairs. Architecture inherits Qwen2.5 with RoPE, SwiGLU, RMSNorm, GQA, and YaRN-based 128K context. At 32B it is the largest dense variant in the Coder family and achieves top-tier scores on HumanEval and McEval multilingual coding benchmarks among open models at the time of release.

Qwen2.5-Coder-7B-Instruct is a 7B instruction-tuned coding model trained on 5.5T tokens, inheriting Qwen2.5's architecture with 131K token context (YaRN). It achieved 37.6% Pass@1 on HumanEval, outperforming CodeStral-22B and DS-Coder-33B-Instruct at release despite its smaller size. Particularly strong on McEval multilingual coding across a wide range of programming languages relative to similarly sized models.

Qwen3.5-122B-A10B is a 122B MoE model with 10B active parameters, released February 24, 2026 as part of the Qwen3.5 medium series. It leads on agentic benchmarks: BFCL-V4 tool use 72.2% (vs GPT-5 mini at 55.5%), BrowseComp 63.8%, and Terminal-Bench 2 at 49.4%. The Qwen3.5 generation uses a Gated Delta Network + sparse MoE hybrid architecture and expanded vocabulary from 150K to 250K tokens for improved multilingual encoding.

Qwen3-Coder-480B-A35B-Instruct is a 480B MoE coding model (35B active, 160 experts, 8 active per token, 62 layers) released July 22, 2025. Trained on 7.5T tokens with 70% code coverage across 358 programming languages; post-training uses long-horizon Agent RL for multi-step tool use. Achieves 69.6% on SWE-bench Verified (comparable to Claude Sonnet 4), supports 256K native context with Yarn extrapolation to 1M tokens.

Qwen3-Next-80B-A3B-Instruct is the instruction-tuned variant of the Qwen3-Next-80B-A3B series, matching performance of Qwen3-235B-A22B-Instruct-2507 on certain benchmarks while offering significant advantages on ultra-long-context tasks up to 256K tokens. Architecture uses Gated DeltaNet + Gated Attention hybrid, extreme-sparsity MoE, and Multi-Token Prediction for inference speed. Qwen3-Coder-Next is built on top of this base.

Qwen3-Next-80B-A3B-Thinking is an 80B MoE model with only 3B active parameters, using a hybrid architecture combining Gated DeltaNet and Gated Attention with high-sparsity MoE and Multi-Token Prediction. It supports context lengths up to 256K tokens. On complex reasoning benchmarks it surpasses Qwen3-30B-A3B-Thinking-2507, Qwen3-32B-Thinking, and Gemini-2.5-Flash-Thinking across multiple evals despite having far fewer active parameters.

Qwen3.5-397B-A17B is the flagship Qwen3.5 model at 397B total parameters and 17B active, released February 16, 2026. It scores 83.6 on LiveCodeBench v6 and 91.3 on AIME26. The Qwen3.5 architecture introduces a Gated Delta Network + sparse MoE hybrid with expanded 250K vocabulary. Community benchmarks show it competitive with top-tier proprietary models on math and coding.

QwQ-32B is a 32B dense reasoning model released March 2025, trained primarily via reinforcement learning with continuous RL scaling demonstrated to close the gap to much larger MoE models. It scores 86% on AIME 2024 and 59% on GPQA Diamond, competing with DeepSeek-R1 and o1-mini on math and science benchmarks. Available open-weight under Apache 2.0; LiveBench shows it landing between R1 and o3-mini at a fraction of inference cost.

7B Japanese-optimised chat LLM continually pre-trained from Mistral-7B-v0.1 with vocabulary extended from 32K to 48K tokens for efficient Japanese tokenisation. Fine-tuned on open-source and hand-crafted chat datasets; released March 2024 under Apache 2.0.

7B Japanese-English instruction-following LLM continually pre-trained from Mistral-7B-v0.1 with an extended 48K Japanese-optimised vocabulary. Achieves 77.3 average on Japanese LM Evaluation Harness benchmarks and 61.3 on English; released March 2024 under Apache 2.0.

23.6B hybrid-reasoning multilingual LLM covering 10 major Indic languages (Hindi, Bengali, Gujarati, Kannada, Malayalam, Marathi, Odia, Punjabi, Tamil, Telugu) plus English and Romanised Indian-language scripts. Built on Mistral-Small-3.1-24B-Base; delivers +20% on Indian language benchmarks and +86% on Romanised GSM-8K.

7B Southeast Asian multilingual LLM (Gemma-7B fine-tune via SFT + RLHF) supporting English, Chinese, Vietnamese, Indonesian, Thai, Malay, Khmer, Lao, Burmese, and Tagalog. Outperforms GPT-3.5 on multilingual SEA benchmarks (MMLU, M3Exam) and achieves 79.0 GSM8K; released April 2024.

1024-dimensional English retrieval embedding model with 335M parameters, fine-tuned from E5-Large-Unsupervised. As of April 2024 it was the top open-source model exceeding 55.9 average MTEB retrieval score among sub-billion-parameter models. English-only; not multilingual.

2B-parameter Multimodal Diffusion Transformer (MMDiT) text-to-image model with three text encoders (OpenCLIP-ViT/G, CLIP-ViT/L, T5-xxl), greatly improving typography, complex prompt understanding, and image quality over SD 2.x. Pre-trained on 1B images with 30M fine-tuning aesthetic samples.

3.5B-parameter latent diffusion text-to-image model generating native 1024×1024 images with a larger UNet, richer cross-attention context, and dual text encoders (CLIP ViT-L and OpenCLIP ViT-bigG). Released July 2023; industry-standard base for fine-tuning and LoRA workflows.

Video diffusion model from Stability AI that animates a single input image into short multi-frame video clips. The SV4D 2.0 variant generates 48 frames (12 video × 4 camera views) at 576×576 for 4D object synthesis; produces higher fidelity and better spatio-temporal consistency than the original SVD.

Step-3.5-Flash is a sparse MoE model with 196.8B total parameters (~11B active) from StepFun, featuring 288 routed + 1 shared expert, Top-8 routing, 45 layers, and a 3:1 Sliding Window Attention ratio (window size 512). It includes an MTP head predicting 4 tokens per forward pass. Achieves AIME 2025: 99.8, SWE-bench Verified: 74.4%, Terminal-Bench 2.0: 51.0%, and 65.27% on Scale AI Research Rubrics. Generation throughput peaks at 350 tok/s; supports 262K context.

Stockmark-2-100B-Instruct is a 100B Japanese-English bilingual transformer (96B active, GQA with 72 query heads and 8 KV heads, 32K context) trained from scratch on ~2T tokens (60% English, 30% Japanese, 10% code) using NVIDIA Megatron-LM. Post-training uses synthetic Japanese data generated by Qwen2.5-32B-Instruct. Achieves 7.87 average on Japanese MT-bench and is available on NVIDIA NIM; the model targets Japanese business and research use cases.

10.7B English LLM using Depth Up-Scaling (DUS) — duplicating and concatenating Mistral-7B layers into a 48-layer architecture — pre-trained on 3T+ tokens. Instruction-tuned variant; topped the Open LLM Leaderboard (avg 74.2) at release in December 2023 and outperforms Mixtral-8x7B-Instruct on several benchmarks.

GLM-4.7 is the foundation model predecessor from Zhipu AI / Z.AI, preceding GLM-5 in the release line-up. It demonstrates improvements in coding, reasoning, and agentic capabilities with stronger long-context understanding and end-to-end task execution over earlier GLM-4 variants. Specific parameter count and architecture details are not publicly disclosed in the available model cards.

GLM-5.1 is a 754B MoE model (40B active) from Zhipu AI / Z.AI, using a GLM + Dense-Sparse-Alternating (DSA) attention architecture. It is designed for agentic engineering tasks and can operate autonomously on a single task for up to 8 hours. Evaluated on SWE-Bench Pro, Terminal-Bench 2.0, AIME 2026, HLE with tools, BrowseComp, and τ³-Bench, outperforming GLM-5 on all major benchmarks.
